summary: Martin Fowler revisits the video store example from his 1999 refactoring book, this time implementing it in modern JavaScript ES6. He demonstrates how to break down a single long function into smaller, well-named functions, then explores four different architectural approaches for organizing the code to support multiple output formats (text and HTML): parameter dispatch, top-level functions, classes, and data transformation. The article shows that refactoring is about improving code in a direction that suits your team's coding style, and JavaScript's flexibility allows multiple valid approaches where Java would naturally push toward OO.
key_points: The first step in any refactoring of a long function is decomposition into smaller, well-named functions using Extract Method, Replace Temp with Query, and eliminating mutable variables

Four distinct organizational patterns emerge: parameter dispatch (format as argument), top-level functions (pure functions with explicit parameters), classes (common context via object instances), and data transformation (intermediate data structure)
Side effects in extracted functions make code hard to reason about - prefer returning values over modifying parent-scoped variables
Top-level functions suffer from repetitive parameter passing that obscures common context; classes and transforms make shared context explicit
Using literal values to dispatch to different behaviors is a code smell - let callers use named functions instead of recreating language dispatch mechanisms
money_quotes: Poorly factored code is a problem because it's hard to understand. Code that's hard to understand is hard to modify, whether to add new features or to debug.
Such side-effects make code hard to reason about, so I alter it so that it has no side-effects in its body.
Using literal values to indicate choice of function is a smell. Instead of this API, let the caller say what they want as part of the function name.
One of the difficulties in programming in JavaScript is that there is little consensus on what is good style.
Small functions, providing they are well-named, can be combined and manipulated to support varied needs both at the same time and over time.
Common contexts suggest grouping logic together, while much of the art of programming is deciding how to separate concerns into a clear set of such contexts.
key_insight: Different programming styles (functional, OO, transformational) are often observably equivalent implementations of the same computation - understanding what unites them (small well-named functions, explicit common context, clear separation of concerns) matters more than dogmatic style preferences.
